metadata.summary: Consistent foundation for Django models with UUIDs, timestamps, soft delete, audit trails. Abstract models, mixins, managers for production Django 5.x+.

content.title: Django Base Model Pattern
content.summary: The Base Model pattern provides a consistent foundation for all Django models with common fields (UUID, timestamps) and behaviors. This methodology covers abstract base models, mixins, custom managers, and advanced patterns for production Django applications.

why.p: All models need a consistent approach to common concerns: unique identifiers for external exposure (UUID vs integer ID), creation/modification timestamps, soft deletes for data preservation, and audit trails for compliance. Building these patterns once and reusing them prevents reimplementation bugs, ensures consistency across your application, and makes it easier to reason about data lifecycle and security implications (like UUID preventing ID enumeration attacks).

list.item: Refactoring legacy models to a consistent base structure.
Building APIs that expose external IDs (never expose auto-increment integers).
Multi-tenant SaaS applications requiring row-level isolation.
Systems requiring audit trails and compliance tracking of changes.
Internal-only models with no external API exposure — base patterns add overhead for minimal benefit.
Throwaway prototype code — the cost of the pattern setup exceeds single-use value.
Legacy monolith with thousands of existing models — migration cost is prohibitive without a feature branch strategy.

section.name: why-base-models
section.p: The Base Model pattern solves fundamental problems that appear in every Django project. Without a base model, every developer invents timestamps independently, some expose integer IDs in APIs, some use CASCADE when PROTECT is safer. The costs accumulate: inconsistency across the app, repeated bugs, and security gaps.

A single base model enforces standards: UUID for external exposure (preventing enumeration), indexed timestamps (enabling efficient time-range queries), consistent `__str__` representation, and DRY manager patterns. This methodology covers not just building the base, but combining it with mixins for soft delete, audit trails, and multi-tenant row scoping.

list.item: Consistency: All models share common fields and behaviors.
DRY: No repeated code for timestamps, UUIDs, soft delete.
Security: UUID exposure prevents ID enumeration attacks.
Audit Trail: Built-in tracking of creation/modification.
Maintainability: Single place to modify common behavior.
Compliance: Audit trail supports regulatory requirements (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR).

section.name: requirements-analysis
section.p: Before implementing a base model, identify your application's needs.

rule.statement: Document: Do all models need timestamps? Do you need external UUIDs? Do you need soft delete? Audit trail? Multi-tenant scoping?
rule.rationale: Not all projects need all features. Understanding requirements prevents over-engineering and unnecessary complexity. A simple TimestampMixin may suffice; a full FullFeaturedModel is overkill for internal services.

checklist.check: Identify whether integer PK with uid field or full UUID PK is required.
Determine if soft delete is a business requirement (can users recover deleted data?).
Check if audit trail is needed for compliance or debugging.
Confirm multi-tenancy needs (single tenant, shared schema, schema-per-tenant).

section.name: abstract-base-model
section.p: The foundation pattern - provides UUID and timestamps to all models.

rule.statement: Define an abstract BaseModel with UUID (uid field), created_at, and updated_at fields. Set class Meta.abstract = True.
rule.rationale: Abstract models don't create database tables; they are inherited by concrete models. This provides a single source of truth for common fields and ensures all models have consistent timestamp and UUID behavior.

rule.statement: Index created_at field for efficient time-range queries and default ordering. Ordering = ['-created_at'] ensures newest first by default.
rule.rationale: Most list views filter or sort by creation time. An indexed created_at prevents full table scans. Setting default ordering makes queries consistent and predictable.

rule.statement: Implement __str__ method to return class name and uid. Never expose integer ID in string representations.
rule.rationale: String representation appears in Django admin, logs, and error messages. Exposing UUID is safe; integer IDs leak enumeration information.

example.title: Abstract base model with UUID and timestamps
example.code:
# core/models.py import uuid from django.db import models
class BaseModel(models.Model): """Abstract base with UUID and timestamps."""
uid = models.UUIDField( default=uuid.uuid4, editable=False, unique=True, db_index=True, ) created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True, db_index=True) updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
class Meta: abstract = True ordering = ['-created_at']
def __str__(self) -> str: return f"{self.__class__.__name__}({self.uid})"

rule.statement: Use UUID as a separate uid field, not as primary key. Keep integer id as primary key for better FK performance and index clustering.
rule.rationale: UUID v4 primary keys cause index fragmentation at scale. An integer PK clusters related inserts and improves FK join performance. The uid field handles external API exposure and prevents ID enumeration. For distributed systems needing true UUID PKs, consider UUID v7 (timestamp-based, coming in Postgres 18) or clustering strategies.

rule.statement: Mark uid field as editable=False and unique=True with db_index=True. Never allow direct UUID modification.
rule.rationale: UIDs should be immutable identifiers. editable=False prevents accidental changes in admin. unique=True and db_index=True ensure they can be efficiently looked up and prevent duplicates.

section.name: timestamp-mixin
section.p: For projects that don't need UUID, a lightweight TimestampMixin provides only created_at and updated_at.

rule.statement: Define TimestampMixin with created_at (auto_now_add=True) and updated_at (auto_now=True), both indexed. Set abstract=True.
rule.rationale: auto_now_add=True sets created_at once at creation; auto_now=True updates updated_at on every save. Both should be indexed for efficient querying.

example.title: Lightweight timestamp mixin

section.name: custom-managers
section.p: Custom managers and querysets allow filtering and querying patterns to be reusable and testable.

rule.statement: Implement custom managers for filtered querysets: ActiveManager, PublishedManager, etc. Use the QuerySet.as_manager() pattern for DRY code.
rule.rationale: Managers encapsulate filter logic so it's not repeated across views and selectors. QuerySet.as_manager() chains custom queryset methods into the manager itself, reducing boilerplate.

example.title: Manager pattern with custom filtering

section.name: soft-delete-pattern
section.p: Soft delete enables "trash" functionality without permanent data loss, allowing restoration of accidentally deleted records and preservation of historical data.

rule.statement: Implement soft delete with a deleted_at DateTimeField. Create a SoftDeleteManager that filters deleted_at__isnull=True by default, and provide AllObjectsManager for admin access.
rule.rationale: The default manager should return only active records, preventing accidental exposure of deleted data. The all_objects manager allows admins and audits to access deleted records when needed.

example.title: Soft delete mixin

section.name: django-5-features
section.p: Django 5.x introduces GeneratedField for computed columns and db_default for database-level defaults.

rule.statement: Use db_default with Now() for database-computed timestamps instead of Python auto_now when database truth is needed.
rule.rationale: Database-computed defaults ensure consistency regardless of timezone settings and prevent timestamp skew between application servers.

example.title: Database-level defaults with db_default
example.code:
from django.db import models from django.db.models.functions import Now
class Article(models.Model): """Using db_default for database-computed defaults."""
title = models.CharField(max_length=200) # Default computed by database, not Python created_at = models.DateTimeField(db_default=Now())

rule.statement: Use GeneratedField for computed columns like full_name or total_amount that should always be in sync with source fields.
rule.rationale: Computed columns stored in the database ensure the value is always up-to-date and can be indexed. Without db_persist=True on PostgreSQL, the field is virtual only.

example.title: Computed columns with GeneratedField
example.code:
from django.db import models from django.db.models import F, Value from django.db.models.functions import Concat
class Person(models.Model): """Using GeneratedField for computed columns."""
first_name = models.CharField(max_length=100) last_name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
# Computed column stored in database full_name = models.GeneratedField( expression=Concat(F('first_name'), Value(' '), F('last_name')), output_field=models.CharField(max_length=201), db_persist=True, # Store in DB (required for PostgreSQL) )
class Order(models.Model): """Boolean computed column."""
quantity = models.IntegerField() price = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2)
total = models.GeneratedField( expression=F('quantity') * F('price'), output_field=models.DecimalField(max_digits=12, decimal_places=2), db_persist=True, )

section.name: multi-tenant-models
section.p: Multi-tenant models require tenant scoping to ensure data isolation between customers.

rule.statement: For shared-schema multi-tenant, add a tenant ForeignKey to every tenant-scoped model. Use a TenantManager that filters by current tenant, and provide an unscoped manager for admin access.
rule.rationale: Row-level filtering via the default manager ensures accidental queries return only the current tenant's data. The unscoped manager is restricted to admin operations to prevent data leaks.

example.title: Tenant-aware model with scoped manager

section.name: audit-trail
section.p: Audit trails track all changes to important data for compliance and debugging.

rule.statement: Use django-simple-history or django-auditlog for audit trail tracking. Add HistoricalRecords to models that need change tracking, and configure user tracking middleware.
rule.rationale: Library-based solutions are battle-tested and handle edge cases like bulk operations, partial updates, and transaction rollbacks. Hand-rolled audit trails almost always have blind spots.

example.title: Audit trail with django-simple-history
example.code:
from django.db import models from simple_history.models import HistoricalRecords
class AuditedModel(models.Model): """Base model with audit trail."""
history = HistoricalRecords(inherit=True)
class Meta: abstract = True
class Contract(AuditedModel): """Contract with full change history."""
title = models.CharField(max_length=200) amount = models.DecimalField(max_digits=12, decimal_places=2) status = models.CharField(max_length=20)
# Access history: # contract.history.all() # All versions # contract.history.most_recent() # Latest # contract.history.as_of(some_datetime) # Version at time

section.name: foreignkey-best-practices
section.p: ForeignKey on_delete behavior determines what happens when a related object is deleted.

rule.statement: Choose on_delete based on the relationship semantics: CASCADE for ownership (Order→OrderItem), PROTECT for ownership of history (User owns Order), SET_NULL for optional references (Task→assigned_user).
rule.rationale: Wrong on_delete behavior causes silent data loss (CASCADE without intent) or cryptic errors (PROTECT without planning). Align the database rule with the business model.

example.title: ForeignKey on_delete selection

section.name: combining-mixins
section.p: Mixins can be combined to create feature-rich base models that avoid boilerplate.

example.title: Full-featured model combining all patterns
example.code:
class FullFeaturedModel( TimestampMixin, SoftDeleteMixin, TenantAwareModel, ): """Combines all mixins for full-featured base."""
uid = models.UUIDField( default=uuid.uuid4, editable=False, unique=True, db_index=True, )
class Meta: abstract = True

section.name: common-mistakes
section.p: Avoid these patterns that introduce bugs into base models:

antipattern.description: Using UUID as primary key without understanding performance impact. UUID v4 keys cause index fragmentation because inserts are random, not sequential.
antipattern.reason: At scale (millions of rows), UUID PKs degrade query performance and waste storage. Keep integer PK, expose uid field for external use.

antipattern.description: Forgetting to index the uid field. UUIDs are unique but not indexed by default in some configurations.
antipattern.reason: Unindexed uid lookups are full table scans. Always add db_index=True to uid fields that will be searched.

antipattern.description: Using CASCADE when PROTECT is safer for ownership relationships like User→Order.
antipattern.reason: CASCADE silently deletes historical data. PROTECT forces explicit handling, preventing accidental data loss.

antipattern.description: Not considering soft delete for important data. Hard deletes cannot be undone.
antipattern.reason: Soft delete is cheap (one DateTimeField) and prevents losing data to user mistakes or bugs.

antipattern.description: Exposing auto-increment IDs in APIs. Integer IDs leak information about system size and enable enumeration attacks.
antipattern.reason: Use uuid field in URLs/APIs. Endpoints like /orders/1, /orders/2 reveal competitor data patterns. UUIDs are opaque.
